lesson.name: Marx: Communism and Political Economy - Lesson 1 — Manifesto of the Communist Party

meta.lessonObjective: Class struggle and the materialist conception of history.

messages.content:
### Summary: Class Struggle and Historical Materialism

This section of *The Communist Manifesto* outlines the foundational theory of historical materialism. The following points summarize the text’s core arguments:

**1. History as Class Struggle**
*   Marx and Engels assert that "the history of all hitherto existing societies is the history of class struggles" (p. 1).
*   Society throughout history has been defined by the opposition between oppressor and oppressed (e.g., lord and serf, guild-master and journeyman). These conflicts inevitably culminate in either a revolutionary change of society or the mutual ruin of the contending classes (p. 2).

**2. The Simplification of Class Antagonism**
*   The modern bourgeois society, which emerged from the ruins of feudalism, has not eliminated class antagonisms but instead replaced old forms of oppression with new ones.
*   Modern history is marked by a simplification of these antagonisms: society is increasingly splitting into two hostile camps: the **Bourgeoisie** (the owners of the means of production) and the **Proletariat** (the modern wage-labourers) (p. 2).

**3. The Revolutionary Role of the Bourgeoisie**
*   The bourgeoisie has played a historically revolutionary role, destroying feudal, patriarchal, and idyllic social relations, replacing them with cold, calculated "cash payment" and self-interest (p. 2).
*   Driven by the need for constantly expanding markets, the bourgeoisie has brought about unprecedented industrial growth and technological innovation, yet has also "drowned the most heavenly ecstasies of religious fervour" in the "icy water of egotistical calculation" (p. 2, 3).

**4. The Seeds of Collapse**
*   The capitalist system is inherently unstable. It periodically suffers from "commercial crises" caused by **over-production**—a situation where there is "too much civilisation, too much means of subsistence" for the existing property relations to handle (p. 4).
*   The bourgeoisie is "like the sorcerer, who is no longer able to control the powers of the nether world whom he has called up by his spells" (p. 4).

**5. The Rise of the Proletariat**
*   As the bourgeoisie develops, it simultaneously develops its own "grave-diggers": the proletariat (p. 4).
*   Through the expansion of industry, workers are concentrated in larger masses, their interests are equalized, and they are eventually organized into a class and a political party through trade unions and increased communication (p. 5).
*   Unlike other classes, which decay and vanish with the rise of modern industry, the proletariat is the only "really revolutionary class" whose mission is to abolish all previous modes of private property (p. 6).

Lesson 1 — Manifesto of the Communist Party - Presentation5 slides
The Communist Manifesto
  • An analysis of class struggle and materialist history.
  • A call to clarify the aims of Communism.

The *Communist Manifesto*, written by Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels, serves as a formal declaration of the views and objectives of the Communist party. The provided text outlines the following core arguments:

**The Nature of History**
The authors contend that the history of all existing society is defined by the struggle between classes. Throughout different historical eras—such as ancient Rome or the Middle Ages—society has been divided into oppressors and the oppressed, resulting in constant social conflict that shifts between evolution and total societal collapse [S.1, S.2].

**The Rise of the Bourgeoisie**
The modern bourgeoisie emerged from the ruins of feudal society, driven by the discovery of new markets and advancements in industry, such as steam power and machinery. As trade and global markets expanded, the bourgeoisie grew in economic power, eventually gaining exclusive political control through the modern representative state, which the authors describe as a committee for managing the affairs of the ruling class [S.2].

**Social Impact of the Bourgeoisie**
The bourgeoisie has revolutionized society by:
* Simplifying complex class antagonisms into a confrontation between two distinct camps: the Bourgeoisie and the Proletariat [S.2].
* Replacing traditional, religious, or patriarchal ties with "naked self-interest" and "cash payment" [S.2].
* Reducing personal dignity and various independent professions (such as physicians and scholars) to the level of paid wage laborers [S.2].
* Transforming human relations into simple money-based exploitation [S.2].

This text provides an analysis of the historical emergence and eventual transformation of the bourgeoisie and the proletariat.

**The Bourgeoisie and Social Transformation (p. 3)**
The bourgeoisie is characterized by constant innovation and the disruption of traditional social bonds. By revolutionizing the instruments of production, this class has moved beyond local or national self-sufficiency, creating a global market and an interdependent world economy. This process "profanes" old traditions and forces society into a state of perpetual change, centralizing property and political power while urbanization diminishes the influence of rural life.

**The Crisis of Over-production (p. 4)**
The system is inherently unstable because it creates productive forces that eventually outgrow its own structure, leading to periodic crises of over-production. These crises result in the destruction of goods and resources, proving the current relations of property are too narrow for the wealth they generate. In attempting to resolve these crises, the bourgeoisie only paves the way for more devastating future failures.

**The Development of the Proletariat (p. 4-5)**
As the bourgeoisie develops, it simultaneously creates the proletariat, the working class. Because labor has become specialized, monotonous, and tied to machinery, the worker is reduced to a commodity. As industrial development progresses, the conflict between the bourgeoisie and the proletariat intensifies. The proletariat evolves from scattered individuals into a organized class and political force, aided by the communication infrastructure developed by industry itself.

**The Dialectics of Class Struggle (p. 5)**
The struggle shifts from localized riots to a national class war. The bourgeoisie inadvertently provides the proletariat with political and educational tools by dragging them into the political arena during various internal conflicts. Ultimately, as the existing social order dissolves, fragments of the ruling class—including intellectuals who grasp the scope of historical change—defect to join the rising revolutionary class, the proletariat.

The provided text outlines the transition from a capitalist societal structure to a communist one, focusing on the historical role and mission of the proletariat.

**The Nature of the Proletariat and the Bourgeoisie**
The proletariat represents the only truly revolutionary class because it is a direct product of modern industry. As industrialization advances, middle-class groups (artisans, peasants, shopkeepers) become reactionary, attempting to maintain their status rather than pushing for systemic change. The text argues that the bourgeoisie is inherently unfit to rule because it can no longer ensure the survival of its workers, who are reduced to pauperism. Consequently, the bourgeoisie is described as producing its own "grave-diggers" (S.6).

**The Role of Communists**
Communists are defined as the most resolute section of the working class. Unlike other groups, they prioritize the common interests of the entire international proletariat. Their objective is the formation of the proletariat into a class, the overthrow of bourgeois supremacy, and the conquest of political power (S.7).

**Abolition of Private Property**
The text distinguishes between personal property—which is based on one's own labor—and bourgeois private property, which serves as a social power used to exploit wage-labor. Communism does not aim to stop individuals from producing for their own subsistence, but rather seeks to end the ability of the ruling class to use capital to subjugate the labor of others. Because capital is a collective product, it must be transformed into common, social property (S.7–S.8).

**Culture, Law, and the Family**
The author challenges the bourgeois assertion that their concepts of freedom, law, and morality are universal. Instead, these are described as products of current economic conditions designed to protect class interests. Critics who fear the destruction of "culture" are actually mourning the loss of a system that trains the masses to function as machines. Similarly, the "bourgeois family" is criticized for being based on capital and private gain, and its transformation is deemed a necessary step in the movement (S.8).

This section of the text outlines the Communist perspective on family, education, nationalism, and morality, while critiquing various forms of non-Marxist socialist thought.

Key Arguments and Objectives

The Community and the Individual: Communists reject the bourgeois notions of family and education, arguing that these structures are currently defined by the interests of the ruling class. They aim to rescue education from this influence and replace private upbringing with social education to benefit all of society (p. 9).

The Status of Women: The text dismisses the bourgeois fear that Communists intend to introduce a legal "community of women," arguing that the bourgeoisie already treats women as instruments of production and engages in hypocritical sexual exploitation. The goal is to abolish the status of women as mere instruments (p. 9).

Nationalism and Ideology: Communists argue that working men have no country and that national antagonisms are already disappearing due to global commerce. They assert that morality, religion, and philosophy are products of current social and economic conditions; therefore, as class antagonisms vanish, these "eternal truths" will also dissolve (p. 9, 10).

Proletarian Revolution: The revolution begins by raising the proletariat to the position of the ruling class. This will involve despotic inroads on property rights and economic reforms, such as graduated income tax, the centralization of credit and transport in state hands, and the free education of children in public schools, eventually leading to a classless society where the free development of each is the condition for the free development of all (p. 10, 11).

Critique of Other Socialisms:

Feudal Socialism: Aristocrats who criticize the bourgeoisie to regain power. Their critique is seen as reactionary and incapable of understanding modern historical development (p. 11).

Petty-Bourgeois Socialism: This school correctly identifies the contradictions of modern production but remains utopian by desiring to restore old means of production and property relations (p. 12).

German or True Socialism: This movement adopted French socialist literature but stripped it of its revolutionary context, turning it into philosophical fantasy. It ultimately became a tool to oppose the liberal movement against feudalism, acting as a reactionary force against bourgeois progress (p. 12, 13).

This section contains the concluding lines of Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels' *The Communist Manifesto*, followed by legal information regarding the distribution of Project Gutenberg eBooks.

The key points are as follows:

1. Political Conclusion: The document asserts that Communists openly call for the violent overthrow of existing social structures. It frames the working class (proletarians) as having nothing to lose but their "chains" and encourages global unity among laborers.

2. Intellectual Property and Licensing: Project Gutenberg provides these works in the public domain within the United States, allowing for free distribution and the creation of derivative works.

3. Trademark Compliance: While the underlying text is free, "Project Gutenberg" is a registered trademark. Users must adhere to specific license terms if they distribute these works commercially or wish to maintain the Project Gutenberg name.

4. Legal Scope: The license notes that while most works are in the public domain in the U.S., readers in other countries must comply with their local copyright laws, as these vary by jurisdiction.

This document outlines the legal and administrative framework for the distribution and management of Project Gutenberg electronic works. The key points are summarized below:

**Distribution Requirements**
Any individual or entity distributing a Project Gutenberg work must include the specified Project Gutenberg license text (S.17). Users must not remove these license terms. If a work is distributed in a format other than the original "Plain Vanilla ASCII," the provider must offer access to the original version at no additional cost (S.17).

**Fees and Commercial Use**
Distribution is generally free. However, one may charge a reasonable fee if they pay a 20% royalty on gross profits to the Project Gutenberg Literary Archive Foundation and provide specific refund and return policies to users (S.17). Any variations to these terms require written permission from the Foundation (S.17).

**Liability and Warranty**
Works are provided "AS-IS." The Foundation, the trademark owner, and distributors disclaim all warranties and liability for damages, including legal fees. The only exception is a limited right of replacement or refund for defective files reported within 90 days of receipt (S.18). Users agree to indemnify the Foundation and its volunteers against all liabilities arising from the distribution or modification of these works (S.18).

**Mission and Foundation**
Project Gutenberg is a volunteer-led initiative dedicated to the free distribution of electronic works for all computer types. It is supported by the Project Gutenberg Literary Archive Foundation, a 501(c)(3) tax-exempt non-profit based in Mississippi. The Foundation relies on public donations to sustain its mission of expanding the collection and maintaining compliance with charitable regulations across the United States (S.18-19).
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: According to the text, how does the transition from feudalism to bourgeois society affect the nature of class struggle?
options: It eliminates class struggle entirely by establishing a meritocracy.
It complicates class struggle by adding more obscure layers of social rank.
It simplifies class struggle by splitting society into two primary, antagonistic camps.
It shifts the focus of struggle from economic relations to purely cultural and religious disputes.
questions.explanation: The text states that the distinctive feature of the bourgeois epoch is that it has simplified class antagonisms, splitting society into two great hostile camps: the Bourgeoisie and the Proletariat.

questions.question: What is the primary factor that drives the constant expansion and revolutionizing of production under the bourgeoisie?
options: The moral desire to lift barbarian nations out of poverty.
The necessity of the state to maintain military superiority.
The innate desire of capitalists to increase the leisure time of the working class.
The need for a constantly expanding market for its products.
questions.explanation: Marx and Engels explain that the bourgeoisie must constantly expand its market to sell products, which forces the constant revolutionizing of production and the establishment of global connections.

questions.question: In the materialist conception of history presented, why does the bourgeoisie eventually become a 'sorcerer' unable to control its own powers?
options: Because the productive forces it created have outgrown the narrow property relations of the capitalist system.
Because the government has become too weak to enforce trade laws.
Because the working class has gained control over the education system.
Because religious institutions are actively working to dismantle industrial production.
questions.explanation: The text compares the bourgeoisie to a sorcerer because the massive productive forces it generated periodically revolt against the existing property relations through crises of over-production, which the system can no longer contain.

questions.question: How does the advancement of modern industry and machinery affect the status of the individual proletarian?
options: It gives the worker more independence by allowing them to work from home.
It increases the worker's manual skill and social value.
It renders the worker an interchangeable appendage to the machine, reducing them to a commodity.
It allows workers to specialize in unique crafts, giving them more leverage against management.
questions.explanation: The text explains that due to machinery and division of labor, work loses its individual character and charm; the laborer becomes an appendage of the machine and is treated as a simple commodity.

questions.question: According to the text, what occurs during the 'commercial crises' of bourgeois society?
options: The government bans all corporations to allow small businesses to flourish.
A destruction of productive forces occurs because there is too much wealth and industry for the system to handle.
The working class temporarily seizes control of the means of production.
The bourgeoisie immediately adopts a planned economy to solve the issue of scarcity.
questions.explanation: The text describes these crises as paradoxes of 'over-production,' where the society faces destruction not because of scarcity, but because there is too much industry and too much civilization for the narrow bourgeois property relations.

cards.front: What does the Communist Manifesto identify as the history of all hitherto existing society?
cards.back: The history of all hitherto existing societies is the history of class struggles.

cards.front: According to the Manifesto, what are the two great hostile camps into which society is increasingly splitting?
How does the Manifesto describe the executive of the modern State?
cards.back: It is but a committee for managing the common affairs of the whole bourgeoisie.

cards.front: What does the bourgeoisie replace all established personal relations with?
What is the condition for the existence of the bourgeoisie?
cards.back: The constant revolutionising of the instruments of production and the relations of production.

cards.front: What distinguishes the modern bourgeois epoch from all earlier ones?
cards.back: Constant revolutionising of production, uninterrupted disturbance of all social conditions, and everlasting uncertainty and agitation.

cards.front: To what does the bourgeois society reduce family relations?
cards.back: To a mere money relation, having torn away the sentimental veil.

cards.front: What is the consequence of the bourgeoisie's need for a constantly expanding market?
cards.back: It must nestle everywhere, settle everywhere, and establish connections everywhere, giving production and consumption a cosmopolitan character.

cards.front: What is described as the 'epidemic' of modern bourgeois society?
cards.back: The epidemic of over-production, where there is too much civilization and too many means of subsistence to be controlled by existing property relations.

cards.front: How are proletarians described in relation to machinery?
cards.back: They have become an appendage of the machine, performing simple, monotonous, and easily acquired tasks.

slides.title: The Communist Manifesto
bullets: An analysis of class struggle and materialist history.
A call to clarify the aims of Communism.
slides.notes: The Manifesto, authored by Marx and Engels, serves as a foundational analysis of historical progression through class conflict.

slides.title: Bourgeois and Proletarians
bullets: All history is the history of class struggle.
Modern society simplifies class antagonisms into two camps: Bourgeoisie and Proletariat.
The bourgeoisie rose through global market expansion.
Constant revolution of production defines the bourgeois epoch.
slides.notes: Marx and Engels argue that the bourgeoisie, while historically revolutionary, has reduced all interpersonal relations to cold economic exchanges.

slides.title: Proletarians and Communists
bullets: The proletariat is the only truly revolutionary class.
Communists represent the common interests of the entire proletariat.
They seek the abolition of bourgeois private property.
The victory of the proletariat is inevitable.
slides.notes: This section explains that Communists do not advocate for sectarian principles, but rather universal proletarian interests in the face of industrial exploitation.

slides.title: Socialist and Communist Literature
bullets: Ideologies reflect the ruling class's material conditions.
National and religious ideas shift with material existence.
Bourgeois values are products of their specific economic epoch.
Class antagonisms determine cultural and intellectual history.
slides.notes: The authors dismiss charges against Communism by noting that consciousness, morality, and law are ultimately derived from underlying economic structures.

slides.title: Position of the Communists
bullets: Communists openly declare their revolutionary aims.
Forceful overthrow of existing conditions is necessary.
Proletarians have nothing to lose but their chains.
Working men of all countries, unite!
slides.notes: The Manifesto concludes with a rousing call to action, demanding the global solidarity of the working class against existing societal structures.
